data.title: Touchstone Balanced Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Recap

Uncertainty that rattled investors in the first quarter continued into the second quarter. A peak in tariff escalation occurred on April 2 (nicknamed Liberation Day) with the rollout of reciprocal tariffs across all trading partners. A brief reprieve followed, easing near-term market pressure but offering little clarity for businesses and consumers evaluating future expenditures and hiring plans.

Despite these headwinds, the economy showed resilience. Retail sales grew, though they exhibited some volatility due to preemptive buying ahead of tariff implementation. The labor market remained healthy, with job growth and real wage increases supporting consumers. Yet, a rising personal savings rate hinted at caution in response to ongoing policy risks. Housing remained a soft spot due to continued affordability challenges as both mortgage rates and home prices stayed elevated.

Markets were volatile but ended the quarter on a positive note. The S&P 500 briefly touched bear market territory before rebounding to record highs. Credit spreads followed a similar pattern initially widening, then tightening to levels below historical averages. This improving sentiment, coupled with eased inflation concerns, contributed to a steeper yield curve, while the 10-year Treasury yield remained relatively stable.

Portfolio Review

The Touchstone Balanced Fund (Class A Shares, Load Waived) outperformed its blended benchmark, the 60% Russell 1000 Index and 40% Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, for the quarter ended June 30, 2025.

The Fund benefitted from its allocation decisions during the quarter, particularly its higher equity exposure, which outperformed fixed income. The Fund began the quarter modestly overweight in equities and increased that exposure further in April as markets temporarily declined.

On the equity side, selection detracted from results relative to the benchmark. In particular, holdings in Health Care and Information Technology (IT sector) weighed on performance. An overweight to UnitedHealth Group Inc. (UNH) (Health Care sector) was a key detractor, as the stock underperformed. In contrast, underweight positions in Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) (IT sector) and Nvidia Corp. (NVDA) (IT sector) also detracted as those names advanced more strongly.

Fixed Income allocations contributed positively. The Fund took advantage of spread widening in April to add high yield corporates, which later performed well as spreads tightened. An overweight in Investment Grade corporates and strategic interest rate management also enhanced results, as the Fund navigated shifting growth and inflation expectations.

Positioning changes during the quarter included reallocating excess cash to equities in April and initiating a position in Las Vegas Sands Corp. (LVS) (Consumer Discretionary sector), while exiting positions in Alibaba (Consumer Discretionary sector) and FedEx (Industrials sector). In fixed income, beyond the high yield addition, duration positioning was tactically adjusted in response to rate volatility. The Fund maintained overweight positions in Investment Grade Credit and Securitized sectors, emphasizing quality and liquidity.

Outlook and Conclusion

The Fund continues to carry a slight equity overweight, balancing opportunity with risk as markets adjust to rich valuations and growing macroeconomic headwinds. While equity valuations have returned to elevated levels after a sharp rebound, the Fund is positioning with a moderately defensive stance while remaining ready to capture selective opportunities.

In equities, the Fund is overweight in Communication Services and Health Care, while underweight in Utilities, Industrials, and Consumer Discretionary. The focus remains on high-quality businesses with strong competitive advantages and sustainable returns on capital.

In fixed income, the Fund holds an underweight in U.S. Government securities and an overweight in Investment Grade Credit, with continued emphasis on high-quality issuers. The addition of high yield corporates in April reflects a tactical response to market dislocations, and the portfolio remains positioned with neutral duration relative to the benchmark.

Looking forward, expectations for economic growth in 2025 and 2026 have been lowered amid persistent uncertainty.

Still, the passage of a new reconciliation bill, referred to as One Big, Beautiful Bill, may help lift near-term activity through tax cuts and investment incentives.

The Fund is well-prepared to navigate a challenging environment. Should conditions stabilize, exposure to equities and credit sectors is expected to benefit. If volatility persists or growth slows further, the Fund is positioned to take advantage of market dislocations, supported by its high-conviction, bottom-up approach that seeks strong risk-adjusted opportunities across sectors.

Fort Washington is a member of Western & Southern Financial Group

Performance data quoted represents past performance, which is no guarantee of future results. The investment return and principal value of an investment in the Fund will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost.

A Word About Risk

The Fund invests in equities which are subject to market volatility and loss. The Fund invests in stocks of large-cap companies which may be unable to respond quickly to new competitive challenges. The Fund invests in stocks of mid-cap companies which may be subject to more erratic market movements than stocks of larger, more established companies. The Fund invests in foreign securities, including depositary receipts, such as American Depositary Receipts, Global Depositary Receipts, and European Depositary Receipts, which carry the associated risks of economic and political instability, market liquidity, currency volatility and accounting standards that differ from those of U.S. markets and may offer less protection to investors. The Fund invests in emerging markets securities which are more likely to experience turmoil or rapid changes in market or economic conditions than developed countries. The Fund invests in preferred stocks which are relegated below bonds for payment should the issuer be liquidated. If interest rates rise, the fixed dividend on preferred stocks may be less attractive, causing their price to decline. The Fund invests in fixed-income securities which can experience reduced liquidity during certain market events, lose their value as interest rates rise and are subject to credit risk which is the risk of deterioration in the financial condition of an issuer and/or general economic conditions that can cause the issuer to not make timely payments of principal and interest also causing the securities to decline in value and an investor can lose principal. The Fund invests in investment grade debt securities which may be downgraded by a Nationally Recognized Statistical Rating Organization (NRSRO) to below investment grade status. The Fund invests in non-investment grade debt securities which are considered speculative with respect to the issuers’ ability to make timely payments of interest and principal, may lack liquidity and has had more frequent and larger price changes than other debt securities. The Fund invests in U.S. government agency securities which are neither issued nor guaranteed by the U.S. Treasury and are not guaranteed against price movements due to changing interest rates. The Fund invests in mortgage-backed securities and asset-backed securities which are subject to the risks of prepayment, defaults, changing interest rates and at times, the financial condition of the issuer. The Fund invests in derivatives and securities such as futures contracts, options, forward contracts and swap agreements. Derivatives can be highly volatile, illiquid and difficult to value, subject to counterparty and leverage risks and there is risk that changes in the value of a derivative held by the Fund will not correlate with the Fund’s other investments. Leverage can create an interest expense that may lower the Fund’s overall returns. There can be no guarantee that a leveraging strategy will be successful. Gains or losses from speculative positions in a derivative may be much greater than the original cost and potential losses may be substantial. The Fund invests in mortgage dollar rolls which involve increased risk and volatility, as the securities the Fund is required to repurchase may be worth less than the securities that the Fund originally held. The Fund may experience higher portfolio turnover which may lead to increased fund expenses, lower investment returns and higher short-term capital gains taxable to shareholders. Events in the U.S. and global financial markets, including actions taken to stimulate or stabilize economic growth may at times result in unusually high market volatility, which could negatively impact Fund performance and cause it to experience illiquidity, shareholder redemptions, or other potentially adverse effects. Banks and financial services companies could suffer losses if interest rates rise or economic conditions deteriorate. Current and future portfolio holdings are subject to change. The Adviser engages a sub-adviser to make investment decisions for the Fund’s portfolio; it may be unable to identify and retain a sub-adviser who achieves superior investment returns relative to other similar sub-advisers.

data.title: Marriott International: Shares Now Better Value As Demand Concerns Weigh On Sentiment (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Like so many consumer-facing stocks, sentiment towards global lodging giant Marriott International (NASDAQ:MAR) appears to have shifted somewhat in recent quarters. Despite tracking for solid earnings per share growth, shares of the Maryland-based hotelier have fallen by around 6% since my last update in November, trailing the wider market by roughly 15 points.

Looking back, my only real issue with Marriott was its valuation, as the bout of post-election exuberance saw its shares gain over 10% in November alone. With that sending its price-to-earnings ratio over the 30x mark, prospective buyers were facing a real risk from a deflating valuation multiple - enough to offset the solid double-digit growth offered by its underlying business.

I do think these shares look better value today. The combination of a lower stock price and solid earnings growth has seen Marriott's P/E retreat back into the mid-20s area, offering investors a better chance of realizing reasonable investment returns. Evidence of softening demand in the U.S. is a risk to consider, but for the long-term investor, I would be inclined to see this as an opportunity.

Signs Of Softer Domestic Demand Emerge

While Marriott is a global business, the U.S. and Canada still collectively account for over 1 million, or ~62%, of its total rooms. And readers will not need reminding that these countries have seen a significant, tariff-driven shift in economic sentiment in the past couple of quarters.

That shift does appear to be creeping into Marriott's financial results. In the first half of the year, the company saw revenue per available room ('RevPAR') rise by 1.6% in the U.S. & Canada, a deceleration on the ~3% growth booked in H1 2024. RevPAR is a function of hotel occupancy levels and the average rate per room. The latter was still rising in H1, but occupancy levels declined, signaling weaker demand.

While economic uncertainty was already increasing in Q1, it obviously jumped considerably after April's "Liberation Day" tariff announcement. As a result, Q2 RevPAR growth was markedly weaker, coming in flat in the U.S. & Canada.

Positively, RevPAR outside of North America remains much more robust, growing by a healthy 5.3%, with occupancy and the average room rate both increasing. Still, given North America accounts for such a large share of its room count, the weaker performance there has caused management to downgrade guidance, with firm-wide RevPAR now seen rising 2% this year at the mid-point, down 100bps on initial 2025 guidance.

Earnings Are Still Growing

While the company is facing a slightly softer outlook than management envisioned at the start of the year, we should put this into some context. There is still plenty to be bullish about here. For one, RevPAR is still growing, albeit it is tracking below my estimate of its long-run average (around 3.5%, in line with nominal GDP growth).

Then there are the other drivers of Marriott's earnings to consider. Less than 1% of its rooms are in hotels that are owned or leased by the firm itself. It is really is fee-based business. This means that, in addition to RevPAR, earnings will largely be driven by the total number of rooms in its system. System growth remains robust, with net room growth clocking in at 4.7% year-on-year last quarter. Like other branded players, this implies that Marriott is continuing to gain market share, and this was confirmed by management on the Q2 earnings call.

A number of reasons explain why franchisees are attracted to branded players like Marriott, including their above-average occupancy rates, which is aided by their significant, growing loyalty programs (Marriott's has nearly 250 million members, up from around 210 million a year ago). Direct booking capabilities are another significant driver, as this results in cheaper customer acquisition costs. Put simply, while franchisees do have to sacrifice a cut of room revenue, it still makes financial sense for the, to partner with a strong brand like Marriott.

To that end, Marriott's room pipeline continues to grow. It stood at over 590,000 rooms at the end of last quarter, around 40% of which are already under construction. At around 33% of its existing system size, Marriott's current pipeline could sustain a ~4% annual growth rate for around 8 years, all else equal.

Cost leverage is another attractive feature of Marriott's business, as a growing fee stream does not require similar growth in operating expenses. This results in margin expansion over time. Indeed, at 65% last quarter, Marriott's adjusted operating income margin is now around 1,000bps higher than it was back in 2019. The upshot of this is that earnings tend to grow faster than revenue.

This was on display in Q2. While gross fee revenue rose 4% year-on-year to $1.4 billion, adjusted EBITDA growth was around 290 basis points higher, clocking in at nearly 7%. As a result, and even though guidance has been tightened, management still expects $5.35 billion in full-year EBITDA. That would be around 7.5% higher than it posted in 2024.

Finally, there is the capital-light nature of the fee business to consider. Being mainly a franchisor, Marriott doesn't have to invest much in order to grow its earnings. Hotel construction costs, for example, are paid by the property owners rather than Marriott. With that, full-year investment spending is seen at around $1.4 billion, equal to roughly 25% of its adjusted EBITDA, and note that this figure includes a $355 million acquisition.

Fundamentally, this allows Marriott to spend heavily on capital returns without compromising its growth. Indeed, management is guiding for roughly $4 billion in capital returns this year. That equates to a yield of around 5.5% based on the company's current market value.

The Valuation

Marriott trades for $267 per share as I type, around 7% lower on my last piece. Back then, Marriott was trading for around 31x then-2024 consensus EPS. I said I would be willing to pay around 25x for the stock. This has basically occurred, with the combination of higher earnings and a lower stock price leaving the shares at ~26.5x consensus 2025 EPS.

To be clear, this is still on the high side compared to its pre-pandemic range. COVID hit Marriott's earnings pretty hard, so I like to look at 2018-2019 figures for a cleaner insight. The company made $5.56 (2018) and $5.92 (2019) in adjusted EPS in those years, and as you can see, the stock price averaged around $130. This implies Marriott was trading on a P/E of roughly 22.5x.

Although its current multiple is higher than this, the key point is that its valuation can now absorb a modest amount of de-rating. Looking at consensus, analysts basically see EPS growing at a double-digit clip for years to come. Given the firm's growth drivers (RevPAR growth, system growth, operating leverage and buybacks), this seems reasonable. In addition, Marriott does pay a modest quarterly dividend of $0.67 per share, equal to a yield of 1%. That pushes 'business-level' returns comfortably above a 10% annualized rate, affording investors a margin of safety in the event of modest long-term P/E contraction. On that basis, I am shifting my rating to 'Buy'.

data.title: Touchstone Active Bond Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Recap

Market uncertainty carried over from the first quarter into the second, driven by geopolitical and trade policy developments. The most notable catalyst was the escalation of reciprocal tariffs in early April referred to as “Liberation Day.” A brief resolution followed, easing near-term fears but failing to bring lasting clarity to consumers and businesses navigating capital spending and hiring decisions.

Despite volatility, the economic backdrop remained stable. Retail activity showed signs of resilience, though this was somewhat distorted by front-loading behavior ahead of tariff implementation. Labor market conditions remained healthy, and real wage gains supported consumers. However, the rising personal savings rate suggested growing caution. The housing sector continued to struggle under the weight of high mortgage rates and affordability constraints. Financial markets finished the quarter on firmer footing, with equities recovering to record levels and credit spreads tightening after an initial spike. Inflation expectations eased slightly, prompting a steepening of the yield curve even as the 10-year Treasury yield remained relatively stable.

Portfolio Review

The Touchstone Active Bond Fund (Class A Shares, Load-Waived) outperformed its benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, for the quarter ended June 30, 2025.

The Fund outperformed its benchmark during the quarter, with results driven by positioning across sectors, tactical interest rate exposure, and security selection. The most impactful decision was the addition of high yield corporate exposure in April, implemented amid spread widening tied to trade uncertainty. As risk sentiment improved and spreads retraced, this allocation enhanced returns. Maintaining an overweight to investment grade corporates also supported performance.

Within securitized assets, positioning in collateralized mortgage obligations (CMOs) added value relative to agency passthroughs. Broader security selection across the securitized space, particularly within higher-quality segments, further contributed to the Fund’s outperformance.

The Fund’s tactical approach to interest rate exposure was another source of positive relative performance. Rate volatility was elevated during the quarter as expectations for future policy actions shifted. The Fund began the quarter long duration, then adjusted exposures in response to evolving inflation and growth forecasts. These changes aligned well with the steepening yield curve and helped capture relative gains. By quarter-end, the Fund returned to a neutral duration posture, reflecting a belief that longer rates are now within a fair value range. On a yield curve basis, positioning remained largely neutral, though tactical adjustments during the period added modest value as the curve steepened in response to shifting rate cut expectations.

The Fund’s sector profile also evolved, with the high yield corporate allocation increasing the Fund’s risk budget to 40%. This addition was offset in part by reduced Treasury exposure. Otherwise, the portfolio remained focused on bottom-up selection opportunities across core sectors.

Outlook and Conclusion

The Fund is maintaining a modest risk overweight in light of current valuations and market dynamics. Although spreads have narrowed and valuations remain tight relative to historical levels, a selective approach still reveals opportunities within credit sectors. The Fund continues to favor Investment Grade Credit, with a bias toward liquid, higher-quality issuers and selective exposure in areas like midstream and banking.

Within Securitized Products, the Fund remains overweight, concentrating on non-agency commercial mortgage-backed securities and residential mortgage-backed securities.

Although many sectors have returned to tight spread levels, specific opportunities persist, especially within high-quality tranches that offer better risk-adjusted returns.

Emerging market debt exposure remains neutral, as spreads have also tightened and headline volatility continues. The allocation continues to emphasize relative value opportunities within the higher-yielding segments of the emerging market debt space.

The high yield corporate allocation remains intact, though spread tightening has tempered the overall attractiveness. The Fund retains flexibility to adjust exposure as conditions evolve, while maintaining a focus on quality and liquidity.

In terms of rate exposure, the Fund is positioned with neutral duration and curve positioning relative to the benchmark. While volatility is expected to remain elevated, this environment provides opportunities for continued tactical adjustments as new data and policy developments unfold. While economic forecasts for 2025 and 2026 have been revised lower, the recent passage of a federal reconciliation package is expected to provide modest support for near-term activity through tax relief and accelerated capital expenditure incentives. The Fund is positioned to navigate an environment defined by slower growth, elevated volatility, and policy uncertainty. The current credit posture aims to benefit from tighter spreads relative to the benchmark, while allowing room to add risk exposure opportunistically should the economic backdrop deteriorate. The Fund’s emphasis on security selection and tactical duration management remains central to the strategy, offering flexibility to respond to market dislocations while pursuing consistent long-term performance.

Fort Washington is a member of Western & Southern Financial Group

Performance data quoted represents past performance, which is no guarantee of future results. The investment return and principal value of an investment in the Fund will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost.

A Word About Risk

The Fund invests in fixed-income securities which can experience reduced liquidity during certain market events, lose their value as interest rates rise and are subject to credit risk which is the risk of deterioration in the financial condition of an issuer and/or general economic conditions that can cause the issuer to not make timely payments of principal and interest also causing the securities to decline in value and an investor can lose principal. When interest rates rise, the price of debt securities generally falls. Longer term securities are generally more volatile. The Fund invests in investment grade debt securities which may be downgraded by a Nationally Recognized Statistical Rating Organization (NRSRO) to below investment grade status. The Fund invests in mortgage-backed securities and asset-backed securities which are subject to the risks of prepayment, defaults, changing interest rates and at times, the financial condition of the issuer. The Fund invests in non-investment grade debt securities which are considered speculative with respect to the issuers’ ability to make timely payments of interest and principal, may lack liquidity and has had more frequent and larger price changes than other debt securities. The Fund invests in U.S. government agency securities which are neither issued nor guaranteed by the U.S. Treasury and are not guaranteed against price movements due to changing interest rates. The Adviser engages a sub-adviser to make investment decisions for the Fund’s portfolio; it may be unable to identify and retain a sub-adviser who achieves superior investment returns relative to other similar sub-advisers. The Fund invests in derivatives and securities such as forward foreign currency exchange contracts, futures contracts, options and swap agreements. Derivatives can be highly volatile, illiquid and difficult to value, subject to counterparty and leverage risks and there is risk that changes in the value of a derivative held by the Fund will not correlate with the Fund’s other investments. Gains or losses from speculative positions in a derivative may be much greater than the original cost and potential losses may be substantial. The Fund invests in foreign securities which carry the associated risks of economic and political instability, market liquidity, currency volatility and accounting standards that differ from those of U.S. markets and may offer less protection to investors. The Fund invests in emerging markets securities which are more likely to experience turmoil or rapid changes in market or economic conditions than developed countries. The Fund invests in sovereign debt securities which are issued by foreign governments whose respective economies could have an important effect on their ability or willingness to service their debt which could affect the value of the securities. The Fund invests in mortgage dollar rolls which involve increased risk and volatility, as the securities the Fund is required to repurchase may be worth less than the securities that the Fund originally held. The Fund may experience higher portfolio turnover which may lead to increased fund expenses, lower investment returns and higher short-term capital gains taxable to shareholders. The Fund invests in Collateralized Loan Obligations (CLOs) that have risks that largely depend on the type of underlying collateral and risks may include illiquidity, limited active market, the possibility that distributions from collateral securities will be insufficient to make interest or other payments, the potential for a decline in the quality of the collateral, and can bear the risk of default by the loans. Events in the U.S. and global financial markets, including actions taken to stimulate or stabilize economic growth may at times result in unusually high market volatility, which could negatively impact Fund performance and cause it to experience illiquidity, shareholder redemptions, or other potentially adverse effects. Banks and financial services companies could suffer losses if interest rates rise or economic conditions deteriorate. The Fund’s service providers are susceptible to cyber security risks that could result in losses to a Fund and its shareholders. Cyber security incidents could affect issuers in which a Fund invests, thereby causing the Fund’s investments to lose value. Current and future portfolio holdings are subject to change.

data.title: EZBC: Bitcoin's Stagnant Utility Remains A Weakness (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
With yet another month in 2025 complete, we grind increasingly closer to decision time for Bitcoin (BTC-USD) and Bitcoin ETFs like the Franklin Bitcoin ETF (BATS:EZBC). I've covered EZBC for Seeking Alpha in the past and happen to like the fund for its designed purpose; specifically, providing exposure to Bitcoin through what is probably a more familiar financial product for the broader investment community. The fund functions essentially identically to most of the other Bitcoin ETFs in the US market and offers a competitive fee.

To this point, the legitimacy of EZBC as a viable Bitcoin product isn't really the point of this update. We have seen over the last year that EZBC holds up just as well as the market-leading ETF product and is superior to futures-based alternatives. Rather, in this article we will take an updated look at Bitcoin's capital flow story as well as on-chain metrics through August to determine whether or not network fundamentals are showing any meaningful progress.

Capital Flows Now Favor ETH

Of course, on-chain utility is just one part of the story. The demand for BTC from DATs and ETF investors has been enormous in 2025. But it should be noted that investors are now starting to favor ETH over BTC. Consider the change in share of net flow dominance that we just witnessed in August:

Asset (mil) MTD Flows YTD Flows AUM
Bitcoin -$301 $20,797 $166,721
Ethereum $3,955 $12,086 $37,856
Multi-asset -$4.4 $113 $7,619
Solana (SOL-USD) $388.8 $1,240 $3,277
XRP (XRP-USD) $307.0 $1,396 $2,659
Total $4,376 $35,527 $219,876
BTC Dominance -6.9% 58.5% 75.8%
ETH Dominance 90.4% 34.0% 17.2%

Source: CoinShares/Bloomberg, as of August 30th

Through the end of August, full month net flows for Bitcoin were negative by over $300 million. By itself, that isn't a huge problem but the reason I see a potential issue is because the outflow is generally exclusive to Bitcoin. Solana and XRP both saw YTD flows grow dramatically in August. Almost $4 billion was poured into ETH during the month - which was a 50% increase in the year-to-date net flows for ETH from the end of July in just a single month. You can see from the dominance calculations in the table above that BTC's YTD flow dominance has fallen down to 58%. To be sure, August was a large outlier. But it is arguably a continuation of a trend that started in July:

Where ETH net flows were almost at parity with BTC in July, August was decidedly an overweight ETH month. ETH is benefiting from both surging acquisitions by investors as well as improved on-chain fundamentals. Similar to the investment net flow story, Bitcoin's on-chain metrics in August also showed deterioration.

On Chain Metrics Through August

Monthly DAAs continue to trend flat-to-down for Bitcoin with 10.8 million active addresses during August. That was actually up 2.9% year over year but still well below highs from 2021 and 2023. Where DAAs have largely held up against averages through the first 7 months of 2025, transactions have been falling:

Bitcoin Metrics August 2024 July 2025 August 2025 YoY MoM
DAAs* 10.5 10.7 10.8 2.9% 0.9%
Transactions* 18.5 13 14.1 -23.8% 8.5%
Fees* $20.73 $16.42 $13.20 -36.3% -19.6%
Avg Tx Fee $1.08 $1.28 $0.96 -11.1% -25.0%

Source: Token Terminal, *figures in millions

Transactions fell to 14.1 million in August. While that was up 8.5% from July, it was down by 24% year over year; coupled with the 11% dip in average transaction fee, monthly fees paid to miners fell to just $13.2 million in August. That was down 36.3% year over year and just under 20% from July.

The other thing to consider is dollar-denominated transferred value. I've stacked ETH vs BTC transferred value shares in the chart above. After bottoming out at 27% share of transferred value in May, ETH rocketed up to 53% share of transferred value at the end of August. I see two major signals here. First, I believe it is indicative of Ethereum overtaking Bitcoin in an important fundamental metric given the stark differences between the two assets in several other key metrics. And second, it could also be seen as an indication that Bitcoin's top is either close or already here for this cycle. During the 2017 bull run, ETH's share of transferred value peaked at 50%. In 2021 it hit 58%. At 53% currently, I think Bitcoiners would be wise to consider the possibility that the gains have been had for 2025.

Final Thoughts

There is perhaps an argument to be made that on-chain fundamentals don't really matter; that somehow, Bitcoin is a special asset that simply needs to be held to let supply/demand produce higher prices in perpetuity. After all, a major takeaway from the block size war last decade was that narratives can change and Bitcoin is more 'Digital Gold' than a decentralized 'peer to peer' money system. If that is true, then capital flows into the asset might really be the only thing that matters. Yet, I have long argued that assertion is false. And that all of the hoarding through ETFs and/or DATs will ultimately lead to network security issues down the line. Regardless, I've been willing to look beyond my own opinions of Bitcoin and simply follow the market instead. But the market is singing a different tune today.

Where Bitcoin previously dominated all other digital assets through investor net flows, Ethereum is seemingly taken its turn. Importantly, beyond simply benefiting from investment demand, Ethereum has terrific fundamentals as well through usage and stablecoin transfer volume. This is not to say that I'm exiting my Bitcoin position entirely. Frankly, I have Bitcoin that I will likely never sell. But I think Ethereum is the better long term investment at this point in time. After an 82% rally from my initial coverage, I'm downgrading EZBC to 'hold.'

data.title: Martin Midstream Partners: Reaffirms Full Year Guidance, But There Are Some Downside Risks
data.description:
Martin Midstream Partners (NASDAQ:MMLP) reported Q2 2025 results that were relatively similar to Q1 2025 in terms of adjusted EBITDA. It also reaffirmed its full year adjusted EBITDA guidance.

However, Martin's results are typically a bit seasonally weaker during the second half of the year (with Sulfur Services being weak in Q3 due to turnaround season).

Based on Martin's first half results, I believe it is most likely going to fall several million short of its full year adjusted EBITDA target. I have slightly trimmed its estimated value by several percent to $3.25 per unit as a result.

Martin should still be in compliance with its credit facility's maximum total leverage ratio covenant and its 11.5% secured notes due 2028 are trading well above par. It may be able to reduce its interest costs by potentially $10+ million per year by refinancing those notes. The cost to redeem its existing notes drops down to par in February 2027, so that may be a good time to expect refinancing. This would be a positive catalyst for Martin.

Q2 2025 Results

Martin Midstream generated $27.1 million in adjusted EBITDA in Q2 2025, approximately 3% lower than its Q1 2025 adjusted EBITDA and 15% lower than its Q2 2024 adjusted EBITDA.

It continued to reaffirm its full-year guidance of $109.1 million in adjusted EBITDA. Reaching that number would require it to average approximately $27 million per quarter in adjusted EBITDA during the second half of the year.

Despite some sales volumes being pulled forward into Q1 2025 (due to expectations of a price increase), Martin's Sulfur Services segment continued to exceed expectations. Martin noted that its Sulfur Services segment beat its internal projections for both sales volumes and margins. The Sulfur Services segment is already 66% of the way to its full year adjusted EBITDA target. Typically the first half accounts for around 55% of full year adjusted EBITDA for Sulfur Services, as Q3 is typically weaker due to turnaround season. Martin's other segments had more mixed results. It mentioned that equipment repairs reduced cash flow for its marine transportation business, but that land transportation did decently due to operating expenses that were lower-than-expected. That helped offset some rate pressure and kept its land transportation margins solid.

Martin's lubricants business (part of Specialty Products) exceeded expectations, but its grease business was temporarily affected by customer portfolio shifts.

Martin's Terminalling and Storage segment was a bit weaker than expected during Q2 2025 due to increased operating expenses, but Martin believes that performance will improve in 2H 2025.

Debt Reduction

Martin paid down $12.5 million in debt during the first half of 2025 and had 4.2x leverage as of the end of Q2 2025.

Martin's capex is expected to be somewhat higher in 2H 2025, so it may only generate $5 million in 2H 2025 free cash flow if it meets its guidance. This would put its end of year leverage around 4.0x.

Martin's results can often be a bit seasonably weaker in the second half of the year. In 2024, it generated 44% of its full year adjusted EBITDA during the second half of the year. In 2023, it generated 47% of its full year adjusted EBITDA during the second half of that year.

If 2025 is similar to 2023 in terms of that distribution of results between the first and second halves, Martin would generate $49 million in 2H 2025 adjusted EBITDA. That would bring it to $104 million in adjusted EBITDA for the full year. Martin's debt would be roughly unchanged in that scenario, putting its year-end leverage at around 4.2x.

It seems quite likely that Martin will remain in compliance with the 4.5x maximum total leverage ratio for its credit facility. As well, Martin's 11.5% secured notes due 2028 are doing well, trading at 106% of par and yielding under 7% to maturity.

Notes On Valuation

I am slightly trimming my estimate of Martin's value to $3.25 per unit. This is a 3% decrease from my prior estimate of its value. Martin's Q2 2025 results were generally okay and it maintained its full year guidance for $109.1 million in adjusted EBITDA.

However, I believe that Martin is likely to fall a bit short of that full year guidance. Martin's Sulfur Services segment is seasonally weaker in Q3, contributing to the company's second half adjusted EBITDA typically being a bit lower than the first half.

I thus believe that somewhere around $105 million in full year adjusted EBITDA is probably a more reasonable expectation.

I am maintaining my longer-term outlook for Martin's adjusted EBITDA at $110 million per year, and have thus only slightly lowered my estimate of its value for now.

With Martin's 11.5% notes currently yielding under 7% to maturity, there should be a future opportunity for it to refinance those notes with a lower interest rate. Those notes would currently need to be redeemed at 111.5% of par though, so it also seems likely that Martin will wait (potentially until early 2027) to refinance.

Conclusion

Martin Midstream was able to pay down its debt by around $12.5 million during the first half of 2025, but its debt reduction pace will likely slow down during the second half of the year. If it meets its full year guidance, it may be able to pay down its debt by another $5 million in the second half. I believe its adjusted EBITDA will fall several million short of its full year target though.

My longer-term outlook for Martin remains unchanged, but I've reduced its estimated value to $3.25 per unit due to that expected miss around 2025 adjusted EBITDA.

A potential positive future catalyst for Martin would be refinancing its 11.5% secured notes due 2028 at a lower rate, which is something I'd expect for early 2027.

data.title: IQQQ: Underwhelming Performance Compared With Peers
data.description:
Overview

I've covered a ton of different option ETFs over the last two years, and after measuring and comparing the performance of dozens of these funds, I've become more aware of what traits make for a good long-term holding. ProShares Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF (NASDAQ:IQQQ) is still one of the newer funds that aims to provide a high income from exposure to the Nasdaq-100 index.

When I previously covered IQQQ, I issued a hold rating due to the limited operating history. Now that we've seen market environments drastically shift over recent months, I wanted to revisit this fund to discuss its performance and outlook for 2025.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, IQQQ's share price has ended basically flat. The fund experienced a severe decline throughout March alongside the rest of the market, which can be attributed to the uncertainty related to President Trump's tariff proposals. However, the ETF was able to ride the positive momentum of the market over recent months to recover. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up above 15% over the same time frame.

Although the fund has proven that it can partially capture the growth of underlying indexes, I believe that peers offer a more compelling value proposition for investors. There is now an abundance of peer option ETFs so I plan to continue monitoring the performance of these peers over time, so that I can determine which is the best buy-and-hold long-term candidate. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying fund strategy that IQQQ implements to generate the income it needs to support distributions.

Fund Strategy

IQQQ now has total assets under management of approximately $227M. Since inception, IQQQ's total AUM continues to rapidly increase as investors seek a place for double-digit yields. Investors clearly have a growing appetite for high income funds that can still provide exposure to underlying equities that make up an index. I anticipate that IQQQ will continue to see its base AUM increase over time as markets hover near their all time highs. This is because an income ETF may be able to serve as an efficient hedge against a market pullback.

As confirmed by the most recent fact sheet, IQQQ is made up of constituents of the Nasdaq. IQQQ aims to harness the volatility of its holdings by employing a daily covered call strategy to generate income. The fund states that it still aims to capture a meaningful level of price upside, unlike some other option ETFs that are known to erode capital over time. However, IQQQ differentiates itself because it doesn't deploy a traditional option writing strategy. Instead, IQQQ maintains exposure to the sale of daily call options through swap agreements, which is a structure that essentially allows IQQQ to mimic option premiums without writing the options themselves.

Since IQQQ mirrors the Nasdaq, the breakdown by sector is pretty similar to the index. For instance, IQQQ is most heavily allocated to Information Technology companies at 52.91% of assets. This is followed by exposure to Consumer Services and Consumer Discretionary investments, which makes up 15.43% and 13.39% of assets respectively. The combination of these top three sectors account for more than 80% of IQQQ's assets, so investors should anticipate that these sectors will have the greatest impact on the daily price movement of the ETF.

Since IQQQ is made up of constituents of the Nasdaq, we can see many high quality companies as part of its top holdings. For instance, Nvidia (NVDA) is the largest holding at 9.17%. This is followed by exposure to other notable names such as Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), Amazon (AMZN), Broadcom (AVGO), and Meta Platforms (META) just to name a few.

Although IQQQ doesn't deploy a traditional option writing strategy, I believe that the inclusion of swap agreements should cause investors to limit their expectations for the ETF to provide capital appreciation. Despite a unique structure, the growth is still capped and at whatever the selected strike price is. Just to demonstrate this weakness, I pulled in a performance chart of IQQQ since inception. We can see that the share price has only increased by about 2.4%, so investors should anticipate that most of the return will come from the distributions that are paid out.

Performance Comparison

There are now an abundance of different option ETFs that use the Nasdaq and the underlying index. So I thought it would be fair to run a performance comparison against some option ETFs that operate as alternative choices for investors. Since these peers track the same underlying index, many of the holdings will be the same. However, each of these funds have a different way of obtaining the premiums to support the distributions that are paid.

I believe that two of the most compelling peers are:

  • NEOS NASDAQ-100(R) High Income ETF (QQQI): utilizes OTM (out-of-the-money) option writing to generate premiums.
  • Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ): also utilizes OTM options but uses a dynamic strategy that can tweak the frequency in which options are written.

All of the ETFs pay out distributions on a monthly basis, which is great for investors seeking frequent payouts. QQQI has a dividend yield of approximately ~14%, while GPIQ has a much lower dividend yield of ~10%. Therefore, QQQI is the most compelling peer for investors that want to maximize the level of income that can be generated from a position. However, a performance comparison reveals that a higher yield doesn't necessarily translate to a higher total return over a longer time horizon.

In terms of performance, we can see that IQQQ actually underperforms these peers in both price growth and total return. IQQQ's price has increased by 2.5% since inception, while the distributions bring the total return up to nearly 22%. However, QQQI is the best performing choice of the peer group with the highest total return of about 26.7%, while also providing a slightly higher level of capital appreciation compared to IQQQ. However, GPIQ's dynamic option writing strategy allows the ETF to provide a higher level of capital appreciation of the peer group.

Although the performance is still limited, we can come to two different conclusions for investors. While performance may change at some point in the future, I believe that the primary reason for investors to maintain a position in these high-yielding ETFs. Therefore, QQQI may be the best choice for income investors. However, GPIQ may be a better choice for investors that want to better participate in the growth of the underlying index.

When comparing IQQQ to a more traditional ETF like Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ), we can also see one of the vulnerabilities of the fund. The limited growth potential means that IQQQ is very likely to underperform QQQ over the long-term. Even though IQQQ offers a higher yield, the ETF still saw a similar loss during the market decline earlier in the year during March. While that market's reaction to President Trump's tariffs were pretty unique back then, it does make me cautious about IQQQ's resilience during prolonged market declines.

Dividend Risk

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.1806 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 13.3%. As we can see below, IQQQ's actual payouts are wildly variable month over month. This isn't unusual for an option ETF since payouts are determined by factors such as the underlying terms of the swap agreements, the volatility of the holdings, and overall direction of the market. However, the large shifts in payouts makes this a hard fund to recommend for income investors that are looking for a consistent stream of dividend income over time.

Many older investors are starting to use these high-yield option ETFs as a way to pull consistent income from their portfolio every single month. The reliability and consistency of payouts can instill confidence for long-term investors and creates a low stress environment, so that investors don't need to guess what their income will look like next month. From this perspective, IQQQ isn't a good fit for these investors that may depend on the income generated as a way to fuel their lifestyle expenses.

However, some investors may prefer this staggered approach, because it may actually work in favor to the fund's NAV. Some ETFs choose to keep payouts the same every single month, but this may be harmful in a scenario where the fund doesn't actually generate a sufficient level of income from its strategy. In this scenario, the fund's distributions would have a direct negative impact on the underlying NAV over time.

On a more positive note, IQQQ seems to prioritize paying out these distributions in a tax-efficient manner. For instance, the latest section19(a) notice indicates that 93% of the distributions paid YTD were funded by return of capital. Return of capital distributions aren't classified as income and therefore, aren't taxed as such. Instead, a return of capital distribution reduces an investor's cost basis and allows taxes to be deferred until the time of sale.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I still maintain my hold rating on IQQQ due to the underwhelming performance compared to peers and inconsistent dividend payouts over time. While the ETF still gets the job done, I just believe that there are more compelling choices in the market right now. Although IQQQ provides exposure to constituents of the Nasdaq-100, the focus on income through swap agreements means that the overall growth potential is limited. However, IQQQ still exposes investors to the full risk of the downside movements in the market. At least it seems like IQQQ is prioritizing tax-efficient distributions through the use of return of capital.

data.title: Amplify Energy Unlocks Substantial Value With Strategic Asset Sales
data.description:
Amplify Energy Corp. (NYSE:AMPY) has historically been an unfocused upstream oil and gas producer, previously owning five properties with no synergies between them, and with little control over cash flows (due to a majority of operations being from non-operated properties). After an oil pipeline leak in 2021, and a recent attempted merger at less-than-favorable terms for shareholders, the stock has sold off.

Major changes are underway after an activist investor, Clint Coghill, led a campaign to stop an unfavorable merger with Juniper Capital, and has now urged the company to change paths and take serious steps to unlock more value for investors. He shrunk the board from 8 to 5 people, invited in a new CEO with shareholder friendly performance compensation (awarded at $6-10 stock price), immediately sold one property, listed two non-operated properties more for sale, reduced SG&A and is focusing on the owned and operated "Beta" property that has attractive growth opportunities (with recently drilled wells far outperforming the expected yield curve). Clint is pursuing a sensible strategy, and as it has progressed he has been buying shares in the open market at $4. All things considered, I see 50%+ upside from here.

Note: All following DCF/PV figures are based on a perpetual flat oil price of $65, and a gas price of $4.

Background

Today, Amplify owns four properties located across the US:

Beta: An offshore oil field with 2 rigs and 1 processing center, in federal waters, 11 miles from Long Beach, Los Angeles, CA. It's 17,000 acres, 4.1 MBoe/d in production (June 2025 MTD production), 17 MMBOE proved reserves.

(All maps from Investor Presentation Q2 2025:)

Bairoil: This is a tertiary recovery project, a CO₂ flood oil field, in Wyoming. I consists of 7,000 acres, 3.1 MBoe/d in production, 12 MMBoe proved reserves.

East Texas/Northern Louisiana (ETX/NL): These are non-operated East Texas / North Louisiana Oil and Gas fields, covering 180,000 acres, 6.4 MBoe/d in production, 35 MMBoe proved reserves.

Oklahoma (OK): This is a non-operated Oil and Gas fields of 95,000 acres, 4.4 MBoe/d in production, 30 MMBoe in proved reserves.

Production data from the latest 10K 2024, allows us to value their assets properly:

Announced Strategic Sale of Two Major Assets

In July of 2025, Amplify chose a new CEO, Dan Furbee, who then launched a strategic review, sold one property for $23 million (Eagle Ford) and marketed the last two properties (East Texas/Northern Louisiana, and Oklahoma) for sale. The value of these properties alone is close to the enterprise value of the business.

Here some recent comparable sales of similar assets in the same basin.

Oklahoma has seen the sale of Conoco assets to Flywheel energy at $33.33 per flowing barrel equivalent, and Paloma sell assets to flywheel at $25.47 per flowing barrel equivalent.

In East Texas/ Louisiana, Aethon energy acquired Tellurian natural gas assets at $15.60 per flowing barrel, and Citadel Energy bought Paloma's natural gas assets at $17.14 per flowing barrel equivalent.

Based on these comps we should expect the OK assets to sell for around $29.40 per flowing (MBoe/d) and the ETX assets to sell for around $16.37 per flowing (MBoe/d).

That gets me to a value of $129 million for the OK assets and $104 million for the ETX/NL assets, or $233 million in total. Considering that AMPY has assets with significantly more higher liquid reserves and production, I think that selling at a premium to this is likely.

I can't see the inputs of managements PV-10 model, but in their investor materials they value their assets for sale using PV-10 at $185 million and $155 million in PV-10 at a fixed oil price of $65 and a fixed gas price of $4 (or $272 million all together, after a 20% haircut). When I recreate a PV-10 myself, I get to $189 and $198 million respectively (or $310 million with a 20% haircut).

My PV-10 model was based on the average 5% production decline, 6% production taxes, fixed LOE, a 10% discount rate, no new growth CapEX, just maintenance CapEX of $10 million and $12 million respectively.

This gives us a range of $233-310 million. For this write-up I will use the low end of that figure.

At AMPY's current enterprise value of $296 million, these properties (plus Eagle Ford) almost cover the current price of the business.

So what's left behind?

RemainCo is Attractive Following The Strategic Asset Sales

After this sale, Amplify is left with the Beta and Bairoil properties. Management values these together at $137 million (using PV-10), and the undeveloped fields at Beta at $133 million.

Bairoil doesn't have much upside. It is a tertiary recovery asset that can run for decades, albeit thinly. It will continue as-is and produce small amounts of cash.

Beta, however, has a few interesting points:

First, over 2024-2025, Amplify added 3 new wells that cost $17.8 million in capital to complete and based on PV-10 will yield $49.7 million in discounted cash flows, 179% return. This exceeds their expected yield curve based on production of 357 Boe/d per well and average drilling cost of $5-6 million.

They will be drilling 2 more wells in 2025, before pausing CapEX. Based on the yield curve, I expect these to add 700 boe/d to the operation by EOY.

Second, breakeven prices for new wells is $33/Bbl, due to minimal incremental costs and low Drilling and Completion Costs (offshore shallow water).

In early 2025, Amplify deferred $15 million of additional Beta drilling due to low commodity prices, which confirms that the 2024 CapEX program was skewed to growth projects.

The unit economics of drilling at today's oil price are good. When I model out 4 new wells a year (for 5 years, using up their remaining PUD), that cost $6 million each and yield 357 Boe/d. It increases the PV-10 value of the well by $300 million.

All of this is to say that I can see where the new CEO is coming from when he says "By simplifying the portfolio and focusing the Company on our Beta and Bairoil assets, I believe we can create a tremendous amount of value for all stakeholders.”

I get the following RemainCo cash flows and balance sheets, which clearly show how value will be unlocked.

Pro-forma Operating Cash Flows

Based on 8.3 MBoe/d production:

Current Balance Sheet

As reported (GAAP, 10-Q Q2 2025)

The asset sales should get us to the following pro-forma balance sheet:

Current Valuation is Attractive

At the current market cap value ($165 million), we find 50% upside from here (or 2x EV/FCF). As using conservative assumptions, you are getting $248 million in value for $165 million. Or, consider that post sale, the EV will be $54 million, and you are buying $26 million in pro-forma cash flows at baseline.

Heavy Insider Buying and Alignment

With a Newly Aligned Stock Comp Plan:

New stock based comp plan for the CEO has the following terms:

Fifty percent (50%) of the Target PRSUs shall vest if the 20-day volume-weighted average closing price (“VWAP”) of a share of Common Stock for the 20 consecutive trading days immediately preceding the end of the Performance Period equals at least $6.00 but less than $8.00, (ii) one hundred percent (100%) of the Target PRSUs shall vest if the 20-day VWAP of a share of Common Stock for the 20 consecutive trading days immediately preceding the end of the Performance Period equals at least $8.00, but less than $10.00, and (iii) two hundred percent (200%) of the Target PRSUs shall vest if the 20-day VWAP of a share of Common Stock for the 20 consecutive trading days immediately preceding the end of the Performance Period equals at least $10.00…The Performance Period is July 22, 2025 to March 31, 2028.

So management has 2.5 years to execute on this strategy.

Upside Optionality

There are positive changes in oil and gas prices, with global demand absorbing extra supply and the likely increase in energy needs and prices for various reasons, from AI to geopolitical conflicts. Carbon sequestration / CCUS credits also provide additional optionality for Bairoil.

Now that Amplify is as a pure oil company, it could be a M&A opportunity for one of the majors. Regarding the sale of Remaining Assets, Clint Coghill is very aligned with shareholders and focused on value creation. As we have seen with the Eagle Ford sale, historically he has sold productive assets at a good price. Once the company is even more subscale, it is very possible that Clint sells the remaining assets.

Further SG&A reductions are also tailwinds; as Clint Coghill stated in his activist letter to management, "Amplify needs to focus on cost structure optimization (noting that Amplify's G&A expenses of $35.9 million significantly exceeds the $11.7 million spent by SandRidge Energy, Inc., a company with a similar enterprise value and BOE/D production)."

Risks

Despite the previously mentioned positive direction that oil prices may go, there is always the risk of negative changes in oil and gas prices. Now that Amplify's commodity mix is 100% oil after the divestitures, this introduces a concentration risk where volatility can have an outsized impact.

There is also pipeline/environmental risk, which the company can mitigates. Its recent environmental incident stands as an example, when a souther California pipeline was struck by an anchor from a commercial ship. The resulting CapEX spending on the pipeline integrity upgrades was costly, as well a $96.5 million environmental settlement. But repairs should keep it safely functioning well from now on.

Key player risk specifically related activist investor Clint is something to watch. He built a business and sold it in 2021, and is now using this proceeds to invest. His net worth may be ~$10-20 million, so he does seem to have conviction here. However, he doesn't have direct experience in the O&G space. On the other hand, as an entrepreneur, he thinks like an owner with skin in the game. That's something that the company could benefit from quite a bit.

And lastly, there is execution risk on asset sales.

Catalysts:

Ten Oaks is running the sales process for the East Texas and Oklahoma assets, they state that bids are due for ETX and OK assets by September 4. Assets sales should close late Q4 2025 or early Q1 2026, and I expect a re-rate then. Management performance period is until March, 2028, so I expect them to continue to move fast.

Changes in the oil trip could materially affect future revenues in either direction. Also, as production from new Beta drilling locations comes online, cash flows realized, and Beta CapEX program restarted.

data.title: Prepare For Some Whipsaw In U.S. Treasuries
data.description:
By Padhraic Garvey, CFA, Regional Head of Research, Americas

We can see the 10yr Treasury yield targeting still lower as an attack on 4% is successful. But that's likely an overshoot to the downside. Higher inflation prints in the coming months will likely cause long-end yields some issues, requiring a significant backup. And we've seen this before. The last time the Fed cut, the 10yr yield rose (Sep-Dec 2024).

There is a path for a lower 10yr rate in the lead up to the first Fed rate cut, but that could be it

Following a week of poor labour market data, we're facing an inevitable rate cut on 17 September. The only debate is whether it will be 25bp or 50bp. We've been here before. In September 2024, the funds rate ceiling was at 5.5%, and it was cut by 50bp. Two more 25bp cuts followed in consecutive meetings, and then there was the pause (we're still on it).

One of the issues of contention from September to December 2024 was the rise in the 10yr yield as the Fed cut rates. There were material falls in the lead up to the first cut. But after that, the 10yr yield ratcheted higher, by some 120bp on aggregate. Hindsight shows there was an overshoot to the downside in the 10yr yield ahead of the first cut.

This is best encapsulated by the 10yr SOFR rate, which bottomed at a lowly 3.15%. That's quite a level when we consider it's not far from the bottom being discounting for the funds rate in the upcoming rate-cutting phase. Roll on then through December 2024, and that 10yr rate was heading to a new peak of 4.3% (or 4.8% for the 10yr Treasury yield).

We sense that something similar can occur in the coming months.

When the Fed cut last time, the 10yr rate commenced a ratchet higher process

The chart shows the 10yr and funds rate through the cut cycle from September to December 2024.

A rising inflation environment in the coming months is a reason for long rates to ultimately back up again

Why the 10yr yield rose from September through December 2024 is open to interpretation, but there was an underpinning of macro resilience, sticky-ish inflation, and lots of talk on fiscal largesse as a medium-term risk. This time around, granted, worries about the economy are more intense. But offsetting this are ongoing fiscal concerns and quite a different inflation dynamic.

When the Fed cut in September 2024, US inflation had eased to the 2.5% area, and had realistic ambitions to continue to edge lower. Fast forward to today, and US inflation is running at around 3%, and likely heading higher in the coming months on account of the tariff impact.

On top of that is the meddling with the Federal Reserve coming from the Trump administration, which is problematic when viewed through the prism of perhaps rates being cut by too much ahead. And the fiscal situation has not improved. Tariff revenue is underwhelming, tax cut costs are coming in 2026, and spending remains broadly unconstrained. These issues pose a risk for subsequent upward pressure on longer tenor yields.

Here's how the 10yr yield can get down to 3.75% and then back up to 4.5%

Currently the 10yr SOFR rate is 3.55%, and it is highly probable that US inflation trends up to the 3.5% area in the coming months. That implies a zero real rate, or the prospect of a negative real rate should the 10yr SOFR rate trend lower from here. Given the market mood, it likely will, partly as the inflation ahead has yet to be proven.

The absolute limit for the 10yr SOFR rate should be the bottom of the rate cut cycle, discounted now at 2.75-3.00%. Realistically, a 50bp spread to that is a fair value floor for the 10yr SOFR rate. That churns out 3.25% as an absolute low. And equates to a 3.75% 10yr Treasury yield (given the 50bp swap spread).

But these are not sustainable levels. It is quite probable that the market begins to run scared of upcoming inflation prints. We agree that the tariff effect is a higher price rather than a sustained higher inflation one. But that too still has to be proven.

If inflation prints in the 3.5% area, we'd suggest it's quite conceivable that the 10yr SOFR rate would have to find some comfort above this level. A backup to the 4% area is quite probable (or 4.5% for the 10yr Treasury yield).

This backs out a 100bp curve from the funds rate to 10yr SOFR or a 150bp curve to the 10yr Treasury yield as a call over the coming months. That's not aggressive for the curve. History shows that a 10yr term premium average of between 150-200bp is about right as a tendency to aim for.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Aave Price Holds At $310 As Bulls Defend Key Support Zone
data.description:
By Jainam Mehta

Aave (AAVE-USD) is currently trading at $310.25, recovering modestly after yesterday’s sell-off but still hovering below the $314–$318 resistance cluster. The token is consolidating just above the $309.5 20-day EMA and testing the 50-day EMA at $312.7, while the 200-day EMA at $315.7 forms the upper boundary of near-term resistance.

If AAVE manages a decisive close above $318, it could retest the $325–$330 zone, while failure to hold the $309–$310 base risks another slide toward the $300 psychological support. The RSI at 48.4 signals neutral momentum, with MACD still in negative territory, suggesting fading buyer conviction.

Technical and on-chain picture

On-chain flows highlight cautious behavior. CoinGlass data shows $2.28M in exchange inflows on September 5, signaling short-term distribution and profit-taking pressure. This contrasts with earlier outflow-led accumulation, suggesting whales remain divided - some reducing exposure while others continue rotating into cold storage. Market cap has stabilized around $4.5B, reflecting resilience compared to smaller DeFi peers despite recent volatility.

Technically, price action is in a make-or-break setup. A sustained close above the $318–$322 zone would signal the beginning of a fresh upward leg, targeting the $330–$332 supply band.

Failure here would lock AAVE inside a $300–$318 consolidation range, where downside breaks risk retesting $290 support. RSI remains neutral, but MACD’s negative slope shows buying conviction is weak, leaving AAVE vulnerable to another correction if buyers cannot reclaim control quickly.

Fundamental positioning and outlook

From a fundamentals standpoint, AAVE continues to reinforce its blue-chip DeFi status. The Ethereum (ETH-USD) beta trade rotation has helped altcoin inflows tied to ETH’s relative strength, while the Aptos deployment of Aave V3 marked its first non-EVM chain expansion, broadening adoption beyond Ethereum-native ecosystems. The Horizon initiative is also noteworthy, designed to onboard tokenized real-world assets and strengthen Aave’s role as a bridge between TradFi and DeFi.

Institutional engagement remains supportive, highlighted by integrations such as Kraken’s white-label lending partnership and liquidity engine adoption. However, risks persist in the near term.

Phishing-related concerns have created temporary unease within the retail community, while regulatory scrutiny of DeFi governance tokens continues to weigh on sentiment. Together, these headwinds temper Aave’s otherwise positive growth trajectory.

Short-term outlook

Looking forward, AAVE’s short-term structure will hinge on whether bulls can clear $318 with conviction. A close above that level opens upside targets at $325–$330, and potentially $340, should flows return.

Conversely, losing the $309–$310 pivot could extend the correction toward $300, where the psychological support will be tested. Below this, $290 is the next critical level to watch. Traders should also monitor Ethereum closely, as AAVE’s 0.82 correlation with ETH means ETH’s direction will likely dictate AAVE’s momentum in the coming sessions.

In earlier analysis, the $318 resistance zone was identified as the level AAVE must break to flip momentum bullish. Current price action continues to respect that resistance, confirming its importance as the defining level for short-term direction.

This material may contain third-party opinions, none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: EUR/USD Breaks Channel As Rate Cut Bets Ramp Up... Potential 370 Pip Rally Incoming?
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

EUR/USD finally got the catalyst it needed to break the channel that price had been holding since the back end of July.

August 22 and September 1 both saw EUR/USD attempt to break beyond the channel, but the move was met with swift selling pressure. This led many to believe that a catalyst may be needed to inspire a breakout, as the pair saw a lot of choppy price action over the last month.

Jobs Report Weighs on the US Dollar

Non farm payrolls rose only about 22k while economists guessed near 75k. There were about 29k upward revisions for the last two months, yet even after adding them, the total still looks like a small miss.

Unemployment edged up to 4.3 percent from 4.2 percent, which was kind of expected, but underemployment, workers who want more hours, climbed faster to 8.1 percent from 7.9 percent.

Hours worked fell roughly to 34.2 per week, and wage growth slipped to 3.7 percent year over year instead of 3.9 percent, so softness appears everywhere.

Over the last two and a half years, almost nine‑tenths of new jobs came from just three fields: government, private health and education, plus leisure‑hospitality.

If you strip those three out, payroll numbers have actually slipped for four straight months, hinting at problems in sectors usually called growth engines of the U.S. economy.

As things stand, serious concerns are starting to be raised by consumers around the health of the US economy. Consumers expect the labor market situation to deteriorate rather than get any better.

According to recent University of Michigan data, 62% of Americans think unemployment will rise over the next 12 months, while only 13% think it will fall. This gives a net reading of 49% who expect unemployment to rise. We've only seen worse readings on four occasions in the past 50 or so years, as seen in the chart below.

Implications for the Fed

Market participants have, for the first time, begun pricing in the probability of a 50 bps rate cut in September. This has been one of the biggest reasons for the US dollar weakness we are seeing today.

According to LSEG data at the time of writing, markets were pricing in around an 11% probability of a 50 bps cut at the September meeting. This has in part led to a slide in the US dollar and seen gold prices attract haven bids.

The uncertainties and the potential for a recession are definitely rising, and this could also be why haven demand has remained strong.

The Fed's Beige Book released this week read grim, and that helped spark a 50 bp cut last year. Yet the current makeup of the Fed looks cautious, and the shadow of tariffs and inflation adds uncertainty. It is likely that a majority for another 50 bps cut won't form, though two or three members could vote for it.

Policy Divergence to Aid the Euro Through December 2025

The euro may also gain an advantage as policy divergence comes into play. Let me explain: the ECB may still cut rates once more this year, but that is about it.

The Fed is now scheduled for two rate cuts and potentially, if the job market worsens, three rate cuts this year, if not an early one in 2026. This sets the euro up handsomely to gain further ground against the greenback.

The Euro Area economy is grappling with its own challenges, though, so the narrative could change. For now, though, the outlook does favor further gains for the euro against the greenback.

Looking Ahead

Market participants will now turn their attention to a slew of high-impact data releases next week from the US.

PPI, CPI, and University of Michigan data will all be released and could have a further impact on rate cut expectations. A softer-than-expected CPI and PPI print could see the bets for a 50 bps rate cut in September rise and further bolster the euro.

Also keep an eye out for comments from Federal Reserve policymakers and how they receive and interpret today's data release.

Technical Analysis - EUR/USD

EUR/USD is breaking the channel on the four-hour chart, which has been in play since late July. We have seen two attempts to break the channel fail, and this time the NFP data may be the catalyst.

A four-hour candle close above the channel has occurred, and if the pattern for channel breaks is to conclude, it could result in a potential 370 pip upside rally. This could put EUR/USD around the 1.2100 mark should it come to fruition.

Now there are a host of fundamental factors discussed above that support the move. There are also a few that do not, so bear that in mind moving forward.

Looking at the period-14 RSI, it is currently in extremely overbought territory. This could lead to a short-term pullback before the bulls continue to move higher.

Looking at the four-hour chart, the swing low at 1.1631 will hold the key for me. A four-hour candle break and close below this level would have me reevaluating the pair. As long as this level remains support, the potential for a rally remains in play.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
